name: Mississippi River (IA)
description:
The Mississippi River forms Iowa's entire eastern border, stretching over 300 miles and providing world-class fishing through the lock and dam system. Each pool created by the dams offers unique fishing opportunities with deep channels, wing dams, and backwater lakes.

Walleye and sauger are the premier species, with the tailwaters below each dam concentrating fish during spring and fall. The extensive backwater lakes and sloughs harbor incredible panfish and largemouth bass populations. Catfish of all three species grow to impressive sizes in the river's nutrient-rich waters.

name: Big Spirit Lake
description:
Big Spirit Lake is Iowa's largest natural lake, located in the Iowa Great Lakes region near the Minnesota border. The glacially-formed lake is the crown jewel of the Okoboji-Spirit Lake chain and a premier walleye fishery.

Walleye fishing is outstanding year-round, with spring and fall being the most productive seasons. Yellow bass provide fast, fun action and are unique to this region. Musky have been stocked and are producing trophy-class fish. The Iowa Great Lakes region is a popular vacation destination with excellent ramp facilities around the lake.

name: West Okoboji Lake
description:
West Okoboji Lake is Iowa's deepest natural lake and one of only three blue-water lakes in the world, renowned for its exceptional water clarity. The glacially-carved lake reaches 136 feet deep and maintains a stunning blue hue.

Smallmouth bass fishing is exceptional along the rocky shoreline and deep structure, with fish regularly exceeding 4 pounds. Walleye are present but challenging to catch in the clear water. Musky stocking has created trophy opportunities. The lake's high water quality and beautiful setting make it Iowa's premier recreational lake.

name: Rathbun Lake
description:
Rathbun Lake is Iowa's second-largest lake, a Corps of Engineers reservoir in south-central Iowa. The lake sits in gently rolling terrain and offers some of the state's best crappie and catfish fishing.

Crappie fishing is legendary, with the spring bite producing huge catches of slab-sized fish around standing timber and brush piles. Channel catfish are abundant and grow to impressive sizes. Walleye fishing has improved through stocking, and white bass runs in the tributaries provide fast spring action. Multiple Corps-maintained ramps provide access.

name: Saylorville Lake
description:
Saylorville Lake is a Corps of Engineers reservoir on the Des Moines River just north of Iowa's capital city. The lake provides the most convenient quality fishing for the Des Moines metro area.

Walleye are the premier target, with the tailwater below the dam producing consistent catches year-round. White bass runs in spring create exciting action throughout the lake. Wiper (hybrid striped bass) stocking has added a powerful, hard-fighting species. Multiple well-maintained ramps around the lake provide easy access.

name: Coralville Lake
description:
Coralville Lake is a Corps of Engineers reservoir on the Iowa River near Iowa City, serving both flood control and recreation. The lake's proximity to the University of Iowa makes it one of eastern Iowa's most popular fishing destinations.

Walleye fishing below the dam in the tailwater is productive year-round. Crappie and bass fishing in the upper arms provide excellent warmwater action during spring and summer. Water levels fluctuate significantly with seasonal flooding, which can dramatically affect fishing patterns. Several ramps provide access when water levels cooperate.

name: Red Rock Lake
description:
Red Rock Lake is Iowa's largest lake, a Corps of Engineers reservoir on the Des Moines River near Pella. The lake's vast acreage and nutrient-rich water support enormous fish populations.

Catfish are king at Red Rock, with channel cats and flatheads growing to trophy sizes in the fertile water. White bass runs in the river arms each spring produce fast and furious action. Walleye fishing has improved through stocking efforts, and wiper provide exciting surface feeding action during summer. Multiple ramps handle the lake's considerable fishing and recreational traffic.

name: Clear Lake (IA)
description:
Clear Lake is a popular natural lake in north-central Iowa, known for its resort-town atmosphere and productive fishery. The lake's shallow, fertile waters support impressive fish populations despite significant recreational pressure.

Walleye are the primary draw, with the lake producing consistent catches through both open-water and ice fishing seasons. Yellow bass are unique to north-central Iowa lakes and provide fast action. Water quality improvements have enhanced the fishery in recent years. The state park and city ramps provide convenient public launch access.
